text.title: Requirements Documentation — Model and EARS Patterns
text.summary: Requirements hierarchy, document-set boundaries, SMART attributes, EARS phrasing patterns, and standard selection rule.

rule.statement: Maintain BRD (BR- prefix), URS (UR- prefix), and SRS/FRD (FR-/NFR- prefix) as separate files with distinct ID namespaces. One requirement per file remains the source-of-truth. Never blend BRD and FRD content into a single document.
rule.rationale: BRD = why (business); URS = what users need (operational); SRS/FRD = what the system shall do (functional + NFR). Teams that produce overlapping content lose traceability between levels.

list.item: Business Requirements (BR-) — why are we doing this? example: "Increase revenue by 20%"
Stakeholder/User Requirements (UR-/SR-) — what do users need? example: "Sales reps need to create quotes faster"
Functional Requirements (FR-) — what shall the system do? example: "The system shall calculate quote total"
Non-Functional Requirements (NFR-) — how shall the system perform? example: "Pages shall load in less than 2 seconds"

rule.statement: Pick one standard per project — IEEE 830-1998 or ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 — and freeze it in constitution.md before any requirement is written. Mixing standards produces section-name skew and audit ambiguity.
rule.rationale: IEEE 830 is more prescriptive on document structure; ISO 29148 is more process-oriented and better for systems engineering. Mixing them silently creates incompatible section numbering.

rule.statement: Every requirement must be: Specific (only one interpretation), Measurable (testable against acceptance criteria), Achievable (technically feasible), Relevant (traces to a business need), and free of subjective terms (fast, user-friendly, easy, robust, intuitive, seamless).
rule.rationale: Subjective terms cannot be tested; a requirement containing them is a deferred conflict with QA.

rule.statement: Write all functional requirements in one of the six EARS patterns. Default to Ubiquitous only when no trigger or state condition applies; always ask whether the requirement is actually event-driven or state-driven first.
rule.rationale: Agents and teams default every FR to Ubiquitous ("The system shall..."), producing unconditional requirements that under-specify state machines and generate ambiguous test conditions.

list.item: Event-driven: "When {trigger}, the system shall {response}." — maps to test: given-when-then
State-driven: "While {state}, the system shall {response}." — maps to test: given-setup-when-then
Optional: "Where {feature included}, the system shall {response}." — feature-flag gated
Unwanted-behaviour: "If {trigger}, then the system shall {response}." — error/exception handling
Complex: combine two patterns; one per sentence

antipattern.description: Using "should", "may", or "might" in an SRS that mandates "shall".
antipattern.reason: RFC 2119 and IEEE 830 conventions differ; an agent trained on RFC documents writes MAY/SHOULD in a "shall"-only SRS. Lint with the conformance schema; reject modal-verb drift.

rule.statement: Version the document, not just the requirements: patch for typos, minor for added requirements, major for scope or baseline changes requiring re-sign-off. Approval block must name role + individual + date + Git tag (e.g., approved-2026-04-15-srs-v1.0). Never edit an Approved document in place; any change must bump version and reset status to Draft.
rule.rationale: Agents editing Approved docs in place silently destroy the audit chain; CI-checked status invariants prevent this.

text.reference: Business Requirements Document template with IEEE-aligned sections
IEEE 830 conformance schema for CI validation
Python validator that fails CI on missing sections and forbidden words
